back_translation: Not long afterward, Jesus went traveling throughout various towns and villages, proclaiming and preaching the good news of the kingdom of God, together with twelve disciples who were with Him.
Along with some women who had been healed from evil spirits and various diseases: namely Mary who was called the Magdalene, from whom seven demons had gone out.
And Joanna the wife of Chuza who was the chief steward of Herod, with Susanna, and many other women, who were ministering to Him and the disciples with their own possessions.
When a great crowd came gathering together, along with people from various towns who traveled to come before Him, He spoke in a parable, saying,
"A man who sowed seed went out to sow his seed. While he was sowing, some fell by the wayside, was trampled, and the birds in the air ate it up.
Some fell upon rocky ground; when it sprouted up it withered away because there was no moisture.
Some fell among the thorn bushes; the thorn bushes sprang up together and choked-and-covered the seed so that it could not grow.
But the part that fell on good soil sprang up and produced a hundredfold crop." When He had said these things, He called out, "Whoever has ears to hear, let him hear."
His disciples respectfully asked Him, what is the meaning of this parable.

He said, "God has granted to you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of God, but for the others We speak in parables, so that ‹when they look they will not see, and when they hear they will not understand.›"
The parable has this meaning: the seed is the word of God.
The seed that fell by the wayside is those who hear, but then the devil comes and snatches away the word from their heart, so that they may not believe and receive salvation.

That which fell on rocky ground is the one who, when he has heard, receives the word with joy, but has no root. He therefore believes only for a short time, and when the time of testing comes, he stops believing.

As for the seed that fell among the thorn bushes, these are those who have heard, but as they go along in life they are choked by worries, wealth, and the pleasures of this life, so that they do not bear ripe fruit.
As for that which fell on good soil, these are those who hear the word with an honest and good heart, and hold fast to it, and bear fruit with perseverance.
No one lights a lamp and then covers it with a vessel, or places it under a bed, but customarily sets it on a lampstand, so that those who come in will see the light.
For there is nothing hidden that will not be revealed, and nothing concealed that will not be known and made manifest.
Therefore, take care how you listen, for whoever has, to him more will be given, and whoever does not have, even what he thinks he has will be taken away from him."
His mother and His younger brothers came to have an audience with Him, but could not get through to Him because of the crowd.
So someone reported to Him, saying, "Your mother and Your younger brothers are standing outside, wishing to have an audience."
But He said in answer to them, "My mother and My younger brothers are those who hear the word of God and act accordingly."
One day, He got into a boat with His disciples and said to them, "Let us cross over to the other side of the lake." So they set out sailing.
While they were sailing along, He fell asleep. Then a fierce windstorm swept down upon the lake; water began entering the boat and they were in danger.
So they came and woke Him up, saying, "Master, Master, we are about to perish!" He got up and rebuked the wind and the waves of water; those things subsided, and the lake became smooth.
He asked them, "Where is your faith?" They were afraid and amazed, saying to one another, "Who then is this man, that even the wind and the water He commands, and they obey Him?"
They sailed and arrived at the region of the Gerasenes, which is on the opposite side from the region of Galilee.

When He disembarked on the shore, a man from that town who was possessed by demons came to meet Him. For a long time this man had not worn clothing, nor lived in a house, but stayed among the burial tombs.
When he saw Jesus, he cried out loudly and fell down before Him, shouting with a loud voice, "Jesus, Son of the Most High God, what do you have to do with me? I beg you, do not torment me."

For Jesus had been commanding the unclean spirit to come out of that man. For that demon had possessed him many times before; though he was bound with chains and shackles and kept under watch, he would break the bonds loose and be driven by the demon into the wilderness.
Jesus asked him, "What is your name?" He answered, "Legion," because many demons had entered this man.
The demons begged Him not to command them to go down into the Abyss.
A large herd of pigs was feeding there on the hillside. The demons therefore begged Him, asking permission to enter into those pigs; and He granted them permission.
The demons came out of that man and entered the herd of pigs; the herd of pigs rushed down from the cliff into the lake, and drowned — the whole herd.
When the pig-herders saw the event, they all fled and went to report it in the town and throughout the countryside.

The people therefore came out to see what had happened, and came to Jesus; they found the man from whom the demons had gone out sitting clothed, with his right mind, at the feet of Jesus. And they became afraid.
Those who had seen the event related to them how the man who had been demon-possessed had been delivered.
All the crowd from the region of the Gerasenes asked Him to depart from them, because they were overwhelmingly afraid. So He got into the boat and returned.
The man from whom the demons had gone out begged to follow Him, but He sent him back home, saying,
"Return to your house, and tell the story of what God has done for you." So the man went and proclaimed throughout the whole town what Jesus had done for him.
When Jesus returned, the crowd welcomed Him, because everyone had been waiting for Him.
Behold, a man named Jairus came; he was a synagogue leader. He came and knelt down at the feet of Jesus, respectfully inviting Him to come to his house,
because his only daughter, about twelve years old, was about to breathe her last. While He was walking along, the crowd was pressing in on Him.
There was a woman who had been sick with a flow of blood for twelve years. She had used up all her means of living on various doctors, but no one could heal her.
She came up behind Him and touched the edge of His garment. At that moment, her blood stopped flowing.
Jesus asked, "Who touched Me?" When everyone denied it, Peter said, "Master, the crowds are pressing and pushing against You."
But Jesus said, "Someone definitely touched Me, for I know that power has gone out from Me."

When that woman saw that she could not conceal it, she came trembling and knelt down at His feet, and explained the reason she had touched Him in front of all the people, and said that she had been healed instantly.
He said to her, "Daughter, your faith has made you well. Go in peace."
While He was still speaking, someone came from the house of the synagogue leader and said, "Your daughter has breathed her last. Do not bother the Teacher any further."
When Jesus heard this, He said in reply to Jairus, "Do not be afraid; only believe, and she will be saved."
When He arrived at the house, He did not allow anyone to enter with Him, except Peter, John, James, and the father and mother of the child.
All the people were weeping and lamenting for the child, but He said, "Do not weep, the child is not dead but is sleeping."
They laughed at Him in ridicule, because they knew that the child was dead.
But He took hold of the child's hand and called out, "Little one, arise!"
The spirit of the child returned to her, and she got up immediately. He then commanded that food be brought for her to eat.
The father and mother of the child were utterly astounded, but He strictly charged them not to tell what had happened to anyone.
